## 7. Discussion

### 7.1 Capital Preservation Without Alpha Generation

§6.6 establishes the mechanism: drawdown collapses through risk bounding, not entry-quality engineering. The interpretive question for this discussion is what such a result *category* — capital preservation without alpha — means for evaluating optimisation systems applied to negative-expectancy strategy classes.

Two implications follow. First, the standard metric set used in the trading-systems literature (PF, session win rate, bust count) is necessary but insufficient for Martingale-family evaluation: under the §6.6 mechanism matrix, all three of those metrics moved against the pipeline while the survivability metrics (worst-case bust magnitude, peak equity usage ratio) moved in its favour. Future evaluations of risk-bounding architectures on this strategy class should report worst-bust dollar magnitude and peak-exposure-as-fraction-of-equity as primary, not auxiliary, metrics; otherwise an architecture that preserves capital will be indistinguishable from one that hands it back to the broker.

Second, the demonstrated result is narrower than the architecture's stated ambition. The pipeline succeeds at the *bounded-loss* problem and is silent on the *positive-expectancy* problem; both are valid targets for regime-aware evolutionary search, but they are different problems with different fitness-landscape geometry. A reader who concludes from §6.1 that regime-aware GA is "the right tool for FX trading" has over-extended the scope. The right reading is narrower: it is a fit for the subclass of trading-system problems whose primary failure mode is exposure-driven and whose alpha source is independent of the optimisation. §7.3 develops the consequences for entry-signal evolution under realistic costs.

### 7.2 Feature Dominance and Strategy Sensitivity

The §6.3 mechanistic finding — that volatility-family features dominate MI because they proxy the strategy's bust-probability driver, not because they predict returns — has two further interpretive consequences worth drawing out.

First, the Müller et al. (1997) asymmetric information-flow argument — long-horizon volatility shapes short-horizon volatility more than the converse — is consistent with the §6.3 multi-scale ranking and grounds why multi-scale aggregated NATR is not redundant with the base NATR. Second, the regime tree segmentation is therefore *strategy-conditioned*: the same 30-feature pool applied to a momentum strategy would likely surface trend-strength features as MI-dominant, and the resulting regime tree would partition the same EUR-USD price history into different leaves. The pipeline's regime taxonomy is an artefact of strategy + features + label, not a property of the market alone (epistemic-harm framing in §7.10 Tier 4).

The four extension features that fell below the MI threshold (vol-of-vol, return skew, return kurtosis, lag-1 autocorrelation) are retained in the pool as diagnostic features and as candidates for instruments with stronger distributional asymmetries than major FX.

### 7.3 The Spread Problem and What the Pipeline Does About It

The depth-linear spread accumulation that drives the §6.5 cost analysis is structural to grid-hedged Martingale strategies on liquid spot FX, not specific to OANDA, the 2-pip EUR-USD environment, or the canonical `'original'` preset. Any execution venue at which spread is charged per trade entry produces the same depth-cost coupling; any strategy whose recovery mechanism opens additional positions with each adverse move pays that coupling. The interpretive consequence is that *no fixed-parameter configuration of this strategy class can be profitable on a major-FX instrument once realistic spread is modelled* — confirmed empirically over the 240-config search reported in §7.8. The §6.6 risk-bounding mechanisms collapse drawdown by avoiding the cost structure rather than by negotiating it.

That distinction shapes how this work's OOS result should be cited. The result **demonstrates** that regime-structured evolution can bound a Martingale's loss envelope under realistic costs without evolving the entry signal at all — Iteration 1's 20-gene genome has no `signal_mode` or `direction_bias`. The result **does not refute** the possibility that an entry-signal-evolving variant (Iteration 2) could cross the PF = 1.0 threshold; that experiment has not been run at full scale (§6.8 confirms architectural readiness; §8.1 commits the work).

For the broader trading-systems research literature the implication is methodological: claims of "evolutionary alpha generation" on FX should be evaluated against the specific combination of underlying entry signal, cost model, and OOS window, because each axis can absorb a substantial fraction of the apparent edge. The present result isolates the *bounded-loss* axis cleanly because the entry signal is held fixed at random; future work that varies the entry-signal axis separately can isolate the alpha contribution if any exists.

### 7.4 Real-Engine Evolution vs Simplified Simulation

The discrepancy between simplified simulation and full-engine evaluation is substantial. Initial experiments using a simplified cycle simulator (without spread, swap, slippage, or margin accounting) produced profit factors above 1.0 and apparent improvements over baseline; the same architecture evaluated on the full execution engine did not reproduce these results. The genomes evolved on the simplified simulator produced extreme parameter values (50-pip hedges with ATR-based TP modes) that created sessions lasting weeks, reducing annual throughput to a handful of cycles. The lesson is direct: parameters evolved on cost-free simulators select for configurations that exploit the simulator's missing constraints, and these configurations are precisely the ones that fail under realistic execution.

Real-engine evaluation also surfaces correctness conditions that simpler simulators render unnecessary — for example, the CFD margin-bust state-leakage and categorical-gene encoding bugs documented in §4.2 and Appendix G. Both produced statistically degenerate fitness distributions (nearly constant values across diverse genomes) rather than obviously wrong outputs, making them difficult to diagnose by population-level metrics alone. The implication for evolutionary system design is that real-engine fitness evaluation demands explicit correctness at the engine–evolver interface; the complete pipeline must be validated end-to-end on synthetic and real data before any full training run is committed. This is one axis along which the present work goes beyond the headline result: it surfaces the boundary conditions under which evolutionary trading systems can be trusted at all.

### 7.5 Comparison with Related Approaches

The engine-controlled comparison in §6.7 (Figures 5–7, Table 5) tests the four pipelines under identical conditions. Beyond the headline metrics, two architectural lessons emerge from the comparison.

**Hand-set rule-based gating fails because the activation thresholds are wrong.** GTSBotPilot's trend-abort module — designed to cut losses at level ≥ 3 — fired zero times across 2,812 OOS cycles despite L3–L5 contributing -$10,704 of pure loss against L0–L2's +$4,992 gross profit (§6.7 Finding 4). The control surface existed but the hand-set activation thresholds were never reached given the actual OOS feature trajectories. IslandPilot's `gate_confidence_min` and `abort_aggressiveness` (per §6.6) are evolved per regime, so the GA discovers operationally meaningful threshold values that the hand-set GTSBot configuration could not. The architectural distinction is therefore not "rule-based vs evolutionary" but "static thresholds vs regime-conditioned learned thresholds" — what Rundo et al. (2019)'s GTSBot leaves on the table is the per-regime learning, not the rule structure itself.

**Discrete preset selection (FinRL family) under-utilises its action space.** The FinRLPilot port to this engine has four trained discrete actions; over 1,170 OOS cycles the Q-policy collapsed onto `conservative` (87.9%), with `aggressive` selected zero times — concentrating on the lowest-leverage preset and still losing capital. The empirical reading is that the algorithm's *expressivity* is not the bottleneck on this strategy/instrument — discrete preset selection at action-space-of-4 collapses to its safest option. The bottleneck is *representational granularity*: IslandPilot's per-regime continuous genome (5 pipeline + 14 strategy across 63 regimes) supplies orders-of-magnitude finer granularity, which is what produces the §6.7 capital-preservation gap. A deeper deep-RL baseline (PPO with continuous actions, or extended FinRL with richer state encoding) is the natural extension (§8.1); the present FinRLPilot result is a *floor*, not a ceiling, on what RL can achieve on this problem.

**Comparison with Yang et al. (2025) and Chideme et al. (2025).** The architectural overlap and distinction (HMM-vs-hierarchical-GMM regime model; GP expressions vs fixed-length parameter vectors; co-evolutionary feedback vs ring sibling migration; problem-derived vs heuristic topology) is detailed in §2.2 and §2.3. Neither prior work evaluates on a live execution engine with realistic spread and margin accounting, so direct numerical comparison against their reported metrics is infeasible. A journal-extension comparison against Yang et al.'s reported numbers under their own evaluation protocol is in scope.

### 7.6 In-Sample Validation and Out-of-Sample Transfer

With a 3-year training window (2022–2024), in-sample performance provides a directional sanity check but not a primary result — the regime tree is fitted to that window, so in-sample numbers reflect how thoroughly the evolved genomes exploit known regime structure. The principal result is the 2025–2026 OOS period (Table 5, §6.1), which tests whether the learned regime-parameter mappings transfer to genuinely unseen conditions.

The §6.1 OOS magnitude is substantial but is not an alpha claim — the PF delta is modest and still leaves PF below unity. The improvement transfers because it rests on structural risk-bounding mechanisms (selectivity, size compression, depth capping; §6.6) rather than on a fragile directional signal. A directional-signal mechanism would be more vulnerable to regime shift between training and evaluation (Bailey et al., 2014); a risk-bounding mechanism generalises as long as the regime tree's feature space recognises the OOS regimes at all — which it does, because the macro/sub feature partition is built on slow-changing volatility and structural features whose distributional properties evolve smoothly across training and evaluation windows.

The 2025–2026 OOS period was structurally challenging: EUR-USD experienced sustained directional moves driven by macroeconomic policy events (US tariffs, Federal Reserve hawkishness, ECB rate convergence) absent from the 2022–2024 training window. Under these conditions the pipeline *survives* (preserves capital) rather than *thrives* (generates alpha); the baseline does not survive. The single-window limitation and walk-forward extension are addressed in §7.7.

### 7.7 Limitations and Scope

Limitations are split into two groups: scope constraints (deliberate boundaries on what was attempted) and reviewer-grade empirical gaps (analyses a reviewer will reasonably ask for, with the specific experiment that would close each).

**Scope constraints.**

*Computational budget.* Full-scale training uses 12,600 evaluations (20 generations × 10 individuals × 63 islands). Larger populations and more generations would explore the per-regime parameter space more thoroughly and are the first axis of future scaling.

*Single instrument and timeframe.* The evaluation is limited to EUR-USD at 5m. The architecture is instrument-agnostic and timeframe-agnostic by construction but generalisability remains empirically open. Instruments with lower spread-to-movement ratios (cryptocurrency futures, ECN forex with 0.3–0.5 pip spreads) may exhibit larger pipeline improvements since spread is not the binding constraint on profitability there.

*Frozen regime tree.* Novel states are forced into the nearest existing leaf via GMM posterior probability, a classification that can be arbitrarily poor when conditions diverge sharply from training (design rationale in §3.6; online-adaptation future work in §8.1).

*Negative result on alpha.* This research does not demonstrate positive alpha on EUR-USD under realistic costs (PF 0.877 < 1.0). The contribution is bounded loss, not generated profit (full framing in §7.1). Reproduction depends on the correctness conditions documented in §4.2 and Appendix G; without them, practitioners will reproduce the statistically degenerate fitness signals encountered in early development rather than the headline numbers.

**Reviewer-grade empirical gaps.** Five gaps are explicitly named here so a reviewer can locate them at a glance. Each lists the gap, the experiment that would close it, and the compute budget that experiment requires.

*G1. Statistical stability of the headline drawdown.* The §6.1 0.75% drawdown is a single-realisation OOS measurement on the cloud-trained genomes. The trained genomes themselves are stochastic functions of the GA's random seed (initial population sampling, tournament selection, mutation noise), so the headline OOS number has an implicit seed-induced distribution that is not characterised. *Closing experiment:* re-run the full Iteration 1 training pipeline with K = 10 distinct seeds (~105 cloud-hours) and report mean ± 95% block-bootstrap CI on max DD over the 15.5-month OOS window. *Status:* not run; deferred to journal extension.

*G2. Multi-window OOS robustness.* The 15.5-month evaluation is one window. The qualitative claim (drawdown well below baseline) is expected to generalise but is not verified across windows. *Closing experiment:* walk-forward protocol with three overlapping 12-month training windows ending at quarterly cutoffs through 2024 and three corresponding 6-month OOS windows in 2025; report DD and PF distributions. CPCV adaptation to per-regime island populations is the more rigorous variant (Lopez de Prado, 2018; Arian et al., 2024). *Compute:* ~30–60 cloud-hours depending on protocol density. *Status:* deferred to journal extension.

*G3. Within-class baseline strawman acknowledgment.* The §6.1 baseline runs the strategy's `'original'` preset with random entry, which is by mathematical construction negative-expectancy on a 2-pip-spread instrument under any directional macro shock — it is therefore a *deliberately weak* within-class reference. The 86.6 pp net-loss gap in Table 5 is real under the engine-controlled comparison, but a meaningful fraction of it reflects how poor a random-entry Martingale is, not how good IslandPilot is. The §6.7 four-pipeline comparison (FinRL, GTSBot, IslandPilot all on the same Martingale substrate) is the more informative reference. *Closing comparison:* a non-Martingale baseline of comparable training compute (e.g., a regime-conditioned mean-reversion or trend-following strategy evolved under the same fitness composite). *Status:* out of dissertation scope; the §1 strategy-class justification stands as the architectural reason this comparison is methodologically distinct rather than apples-to-apples.

*G4. Topology ablation: GA on the alternate regime tree.* §6.3 / Appendix I show ARI = 0.034 between the production fallback tree and the 3-MI-feature tree (Tree A) — they are structurally different partitions of the same data. The downstream PnL consequence is unmeasured. *Closing experiment:* re-run the Iteration 1 GA on Tree A with identical seeds, population, and budget; compare OOS DD, PF, and bust rate against the Tree B production result. *Compute:* ~10 cloud-hours. *Status:* deferred to journal extension; a positive result (Tree A produces a comparable or better OOS) would weaken the "topology-from-regimes is doing the work" claim, while a substantially worse result would strengthen it. The honest answer today is "we have not measured this".

*G5. Flat-island GA control on the same gene space.* The contribution claim "topology-from-regimes" implies that the regime-derived topology is doing work that a flat-island or single-population GA on the same 20-gene parameter space could not. The §6.2 random-search control compares against uniform random sampling, not against a panmictic GA. *Closing experiment:* a single-population GA with N = 630 individuals (matching IslandPilot's total population), 20 generations, evaluating each genome's fitness on the *aggregate* 36-month window rather than per-regime, with the same operators and seeds. Report OOS DD, PF, bust rate. *Compute:* ~10–12 cloud-hours. *Status:* the most reviewer-load-bearing missing comparison; deferred to journal extension. A finding that the panmictic GA achieves comparable OOS would substantially weaken the architectural contribution claim, so this gap should be closed before the journal version.

In-progress validations and direct empirical extensions are listed in §8.1.

### 7.8 Implications for Martingale Strategy Design

The exhaustive 240-configuration fixed-parameter sweep that preceded this work returned no profitable configuration on EUR-USD under realistic OANDA costs over 2022–2024 — confirming the §2.4 structural argument empirically. The interpretive question is what the artefact-verified evolved-parameter pattern (§6.4) implies for the *design space* of adaptive Martingale management more generally.

Two implications follow from the pattern of choices the GA actually made.

**Termination beats truncation.** Given the freedom to evolve `max_levels` anywhere in [2, 6], the GA fixed it at the ceiling for 96.5% of individuals and instead spent its expressive budget on `abort_aggressiveness` variation (range 0.000–0.385). The design lesson is that *when* to terminate the cycle matters more than *how deep* it is allowed to go, at least under this fitness composite. A future Martingale design that hard-codes a low depth ceiling forfeits the regime-conditioned timing information that an evolved abort policy carries; conversely, a design that leaves depth uncapped and relies entirely on a learned abort can plausibly recover the same loss envelope. Whether this is universally true or specific to the §4 fitness composite (which weights bust rate cubically) is an open question worth a targeted ablation.

**Recovery-arithmetic floors are not load-bearing under a working regime gate.** 40% of Iteration 1 genomes evolved `sizing_factor` below the √2 mathematical-viability threshold, yet the §6 capital-preservation result still emerges. The implication is not that the √2 floor is wrong — it is correct under the textbook recovery argument — but that under a sufficiently selective regime gate the infeasible-recovery genomes are simply *not deployed in regimes where their infeasibility binds*. The gate masks the mathematical violation. Iteration 2's explicit [1.5, 2.5] floor is therefore best read as belt-and-braces hardening rather than as a precondition for the result: the gate is the binding safeguard.

Both implications generalise beyond Martingale to any negative-expectancy strategy class managed by a selective adaptation layer: the adaptation layer can substitute for, but does not replace, the underlying mathematical constraints. A practitioner reading this work should not infer that recovery-arithmetic constraints are dispensable in production — only that under a working regime gate the constraints become operationally redundant rather than structurally necessary. Removing the gate exposes them again.

### 7.9 Generalisability Beyond EUR-USD

IslandPilot is instrument-agnostic by construction — the feature pool, regime discovery, and island evolution all operate through standard OHLCV data and the engine's backtesting interface — but the current results are specific to EUR-USD on OANDA. On instruments where execution costs are a smaller fraction of gross profit per session (cryptocurrency futures with ~0.04% taker fees, ECN forex with 0.3–0.5 pip spreads), the base strategy may already operate near breakeven, in which case the pipeline's contribution would shift from loss bounding to genuine return improvement. Whether the pipeline can cross the PF = 1.0 threshold on such instruments is an open empirical question and a natural extension target.

The regime-tree features (NATR, ATR, ADX, session hour, choppiness) apply to any liquid instrument, but the specific regime structure (macro-cluster count, sub-cluster composition, feature importance) will differ. An instrument with strong seasonality might produce trees dominated by calendar features; a high-volatility cryptocurrency pair might produce trees dominated by ATR-derived features. The architecture accommodates these differences automatically through BIC-based model selection at both clustering levels, adapting the regime granularity to each instrument's feature-space statistics. Establishing transfer of the regime-structured island topology across currency pairs (preliminary GBP-USD and USD-JPY work) and across asset classes (crypto, equity indices) is one of the directions the conference-paper extension is designed to investigate (§8.1).

**Macro-event scope.** The 2025–2026 OOS window encompasses specific macroeconomic events (US tariff announcements, Federal Reserve policy shifts, ECB rate convergence) that were not present in the 2022–2024 training window. The pipeline survived these events through *exposure bounding and abort-driven cycle termination* rather than through regime recognition of the novel macro states themselves — the regime tree was not retrained on 2025–2026 data and could not represent these new macro conditions. This supports the robustness of the capital-preservation mechanism (which compounds multiplicatively on the loss path independent of regime-tree accuracy) but does **not** validate the regime tree's macro-regime taxonomy under structural breaks. Online adaptation of the regime tree (§8.1) is the architectural response to this limitation; without it, replication on a window with different structural breaks should expect the same *qualitative* result (drawdown well below the baseline) but not the same *quantitative* number.

### 7.10 Ethics and Responsible-Deployment Considerations

Ethics in this work is treated as a design rationale rather than a post-hoc reflection. Three concrete design decisions short-circuit specific harm chains documented in the trading-systems research literature: production-engine fitness evaluation (§1, §4) addresses the simulator-overstatement chain by trading roughly 10× computational cost for realistic execution conditions; full reporting of unfavourable primary metrics in Table 5 (PF < 1.0, 50% bust rate, 5.6% L0 win rate) refuses the selective-reporting mechanism by which overstated priors propagate into the practitioner community; and the Appendix G correctness-condition disclosure (§4.2) transfers the diagnostic burden for non-obvious, fitness-degenerating engine bugs from the replicator to the original author. Each decision is methodological *and* ethical, and each is justified once at the point of decision rather than rehearsed in this section.

The 10× cost trade-off is worth interrogating analytically rather than asserted as an unalloyed good. The choice was viable here because (a) the project had cloud-credit access to a 60-vCPU spot VM that absorbed the per-evaluation cost into ~10 wall-clock hours; (b) a 20-gene Iteration 1 search space was tractable at this budget; (c) the alternative — discovering simulator-only genomes were unusable months later in deployment — has a higher amortised cost than 10× per-evaluation compute. None of these conditions is universal: a research group without spot-VM access, or working on a 200-gene genome, or under a per-deadline time-box, may face a different trade. The ethical commitment ("evaluate on production-equivalent conditions") is invariant; the *implementation* of that commitment is contingent on what compute the work can absorb. Future work should report the equivalent budget breakdown so the trade-off can be replicated on its own terms rather than presumed transferable.

The research touches a four-tier harm surface; the design or reporting decision that mitigates each tier is summarised below.

*Table 11: Harm tiers and their mitigants.*

| Tier | Harm | Mitigant |
|---|---|---|
| 1 — Individual | Retail trader clones the artefact, encounters a structural break not in the 2022–2024 training data, sustains loss. | Deployment disclaimer (front-matter); explicit PF < 1.0 and single-window framing in §6.1, §7.1, §7.7; demo-account paper-trading recommendation; Appendix G correctness conditions. |
| 2 — Research community | Citers propagate the §6.1 drawdown headline without noting sample size (72 sessions), single 15.5-month window, or absence of significance testing. | §7.7 limitations; Table 4a scope-of-claims map; explicit ask that replicators report all five Table 5 metrics. |
| 3 — Market microstructure | At scale, multi-instrument deployment of correlated instances could move the spread the model assumed — an ML-derived system influencing the prices it depends on. | Out of scope at single-account dissertation evaluation. Production extension responsibility: monitor fill quality, latency, effective-spread asymmetry; pull back if the strategy moves the prices it consumes. |
| 4 — Epistemic | "Regime discovery" misread as identification of *causal* market states; subsequent work cites this paper as having "mapped EUR-USD regime structure." | Hedged language throughout ("the regime tree the pipeline uses", not "the regimes of EUR-USD"); documented sensitivity to feature-set choice (§6.3 / Appendix I, ARI = 0.034 between two valid trees). |

Data governance is addressed in §5.1 (OANDA Developer Programme terms; no personal or counterparty data; release scope through the project repository); the bidirectional generative-AI use declaration appears in front-matter. Live deployment is the operator's regulatory responsibility, not the researcher's, and any reproduction on real capital must be preceded by demo-account paper trading over a comparable horizon — no guarantee of profit, capital preservation, or replicability of the §6 numbers is offered or implied (full disclaimer in front-matter).

---
